comments.title: Chapter 4 of Seed of Yggdrasill and Question on a "Shared Soul"
comments.content:
Good Evening,

Ever since reading Amy Tan's /The Moon Lady/, lavishly illustrated by Gretchen Schields, my older daughter has been very interested in the Chinese Moon Festival, so we enjoyed some Chinese food and moon cakes to mark the occasion. I hope you and yours had something equally nice for the fall's (or spring's) first full moon.

To Share: This week I share further thoughts on Maria Kvilhaug's The Seed of Yggdrasill, this time on chapter 4, which discusses a myth near and dear to my heart, the abduction and return of Idun: https://jpowellrussell.com/#book_the_seed_of_yggdrasill_4_retrieving_the_soul_of_the_gods

To Ask: As my link above indicates, I've been reading Maria Kvilhaug's The Seed of Yggdrasill, which is full of interesting insights, but one of her conclusions seems well-explained enough, but I can't really get what she's talking about, and I wonder if JMG or others might be able to help. Kvilhaug calls herself a pantheist, and tends to see the identities of the different Gods and Goddesses as fairly fluid, and mostly to do with what they're doing literarilly in any given myth. I may have some different ideas, but generally, I get the idea: each God or Goddess is more like a mask or a costume for a particular role, with the same undifferentiated divinity behind it. What I don't understand is when Kvilhaug describes the Goddess Idhun as "the Soul of the Gods." I think I'm tripping up on two main points: 1. If all of the Gods and Goddesses are just masks/aspects of an undifferentiated divinity, then what would a "soul" be separate from that undifferentiated divinity? 2. If a "soul" is something separate from the undifferentiated divinity, what would it mean for multiple Beings (much less multiple "masks") to share a soul?

Clearly, this is a good theme for meditation, and I will be giving it more time as such, but I suspect I'm making one or more category errors in how I'm conceiving of things.

As always, thanks very much to JMG and everyone else here for all that you do.

My blessings and best wishes to all who will have them,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Chapter 4 of Seed of Yggdrasill and Question on a "Shared Soul"
replies.content:
I'm amused to note that Kvilhaug isn't quite unique in using that kind of language. According to H.P. Lovecraft, Nyarlathotep is the soul as well as the mighty messenger of the Great Old Ones! I have to admit, though, that I have no idea what either of them means by that. One of the reasons is that the word "soul" is very vague, and can be used to mean quite a number of things, very few of which are separate personalities or individuals.

Of course the old Germanic concept may be different. Winifred, or anyone else who's up on Heathen soul lore, I'd be interested to hear your comments.

replies.title: Re: Chapter 4 of Seed of Yggdrasill and Question on a "Shared Soul"
replies.content:
Ah, thanks, but that's too bad. I was hoping that she was maybe building on some school of thought or teaching that might be better spelled out elsewhere, but it sounds like it may be yet another case of idiosyncratic use of signs for an already-hard-to-explain-and-understand signified.

I'll welcome anyone else's thoughts, then!

replies.title: Re: Chapter 4 of Seed of Yggdrasill and Question on a "Shared Soul"
replies.content: IIRC, without the steady diet of the apples of immortality that only Idunn can provide, every other Norse Deity would have aged and died (well before Ragnarök), and only she would have remained after their deaths.  In that sense one might well call Idunn the "Soul of the Gods."

replies.title: Re: Chapter 4 of Seed of Yggdrasill and Question on a "Shared Soul"
replies.content: Which raises more questions, because if the Gods are just masks of some unidentified deity, the deity can just put the masks back on after Ragnarok, and presumably the apples aren't even required for their existence.

replies.title: Re: Chapter 4 of Seed of Yggdrasill and Question on a "Shared Soul"
replies.content:
To the best of my understanding, none of the Norse Deities are essentially immortal. They will all die sooner or later, every last one of them. Nor are they mere mortal masks for some immortal Being behind those masks.

The Norse Deities were the first Deities I ever heard about, as part of my Danish-American father's cultural heritage. (He was an agnostic, as were his parents, so they were his cultural heritage only, not his religious heritage.) So my earliest concept of Gods and Goddeses is that they are all mortal Beings. Only later in life did I ever hear anything about the immortal God of Christianity, and I still have trouble wrapping my head around the notion of Divine immortality. It just feels "wrong" somehow ...

replies.title: Re: Chapter 4 of Seed of Yggdrasill and Question on a "Shared Soul"
replies.content:
Kvilhaug has a rather unusual framework for interpreting the myths. The short version is that she considers herself a "pantheist," though I have not yet encountered what specific influences she would point to for that term, since it's famously inconsistently understood and applied. She further believes that the myths of the Edda are more or less all descriptions/reflections of initiatory experiences.

When you put these together, she seems to believe that any given figure in a myth is "standing for" or "symbolizing" various forces or powers, often including inner psychological or spiritual states. She sees the roles of the myths as guides to how a reader should navigate his or her own esoteric spiritual practice. Since she thinks these are all just different aspects of the same underlying divinity, she plays rather fast and loose with their "distinctness" as "persons." For example, at one point, she suggests that Idhunn, Sol, Urdhr, Frigg, Freyja, and others might all be "the same" Goddess.

So, while I don't really buy her overall framework, I am trying to understand this specific claim about "the soul of the Gods" within that framework.

replies.title: Re: Chapter 4 of Seed of Yggdrasill and Question on a "Shared Soul"
replies.content:
I don't expect this to be that helpful, but it reminds me of the unusual wuxia-cross-late-Roman-Empire tabletop RPG Exalted. In that game's setting, each of the titans called "Primordials" has a peculiar structure of souls, which each span the Primordial's being (in one sense, while being independently embodied and vulnerable in a different sense). Sort of like how a corporation has departments, each of which touches on the activities of the rest of the corporation. The souls are usually classified with mysterious titles like "Generative Soul", "Expressive Soul", "Wisdom Soul", "Indulgent Soul", or "Messenger Soul", the most important being the cornerstone "Defining Soul" that constitutes its superordinate entity's identity and basic organization. The human-sympathizing Primordial Gaea has among her souls the presiding elemental dragons for each of the five Taoist elements, which she has sent on permanent loan to help form and stabilize Creation, and their subsouls are thought to be the many various elementally-aspected magical sites within Creation. It's a weird setting.

I bring it up partly because one could imagine relationships that were like that but for multiple entities at once, the way you can have multiple corporations outsource their legal or physical plant departments to the same other corporation, or like how Andreesen Horowitz performs the role of many of a conventional company's non-technical departments for the startups they are funding.

replies.title: Re: Chapter 4 of Seed of Yggdrasill and Question on a "Shared Soul"
replies.content:
Thanks for sharing your reading. It seems "The Moon Lady" is based on Amy Tan's earlier book "The Joy Luck Club". Highly recommended!

One of the meanings assigned to the word "soul" is the force that makes you grow. For example the force that regenerates your skin after you damage it. That seems like a match for Idhun, in whose absence the Norse gods start to age and grow old.

replies.title: Re: Chapter 4 of Seed of Yggdrasill and Question on a "Shared Soul"
replies.content: Thank you, that's interesting! It also reminds me that though Kvilhaug is a fluent speaker of English, it's not her first tongue, and I have read that translating "soul" and adjacent words between Germanic tongues can be tricky due to wildly different usages and connotations ("geist" in German being one of the most infamous). I hadn't really considered that her use of "soul" might be informed by some of those different connotations, if they are wildly different in Norwegian than in English.

replies.title: Re: Chapter 4 of Seed of Yggdrasill and Question on a "Shared Soul"
replies.content:
I might be botching the reference a bit, but I recall Proclus writing that the Gods have the One in the same way that humans have individual souls. Anyway that's what I remember the passage saying, the what would make more sense if it actually said that the Gne is to the gods as the Universal Soul is to individual human beings, and in fact that may be it. (I can't recall the source now, it might be his commentary on the Timaeus.)

If that is the case, then the apples of Idunn which maintain the Gods in immortality are an image of the overflowing creativity which emanates from the One. But here we have a female power standing in for the One, rather than a masculine deity like God the Father, Allah or Ouranos.

Asgard itself would then be the Divine Intellect, which Plotinus compares to a city in any case. But would would be the World Soul? Yggdrasil?

replies.title: Re: Chapter 4 of Seed of Yggdrasill and Question on a "Shared Soul"
replies.content: That doesn't strike me as implausible, but I'm still on the "read through Plato" phase of getting up to speed on the Neoplatonists, so I might not know enough to comment intelligently! Yggdrasill tends to strike me as importantly somehow the "body" of living cosmos, for what that's worth. Perhaps one (or more) of the Wells from which Yggdrasill draws its sustenance? Urdharbrunnr/The Well of Wyrd strikes me as the most likely of the three.

replies.title: Re: Chapter 4 of Seed of Yggdrasill and Question on a "Shared Soul"
replies.content: My own understanding of Proclus is that each God IS One, in that each God is individuated wholly as Themselves, that each God has no parts, and as each God has no temporal cause.  While a God might have a "father" or "mother", this describes where the "son" or "daughter" deity carries on the work of the parent further down the chain of being without existing as beings Themselves as each precedes Being in Their existence as whole units, or henads.

replies.title: Re: Chapter 4 of Seed of Yggdrasill and Question on a "Shared Soul"
replies.content: How old is your daughter? She would likely enjoy Grace Lin’s Where the Mountain Meets the Moon trilogy. Beautifully illustrated novels based on Chinese folklore. The reading level is broad; my 5-year-old understood them perfectly well and begged for chapter after chapter. They were published around 10 years ago and there’s zero political propaganda/no ideological undercurrent to them. They are really wholesome and lovely. (I can’t vouch for Lin’s other novels though, some seem lije they might be pushing various agendas.)

replies.title: Re: Chapter 4 of Seed of Yggdrasill and Question on a "Shared Soul"
replies.content: Thank you for this! She's almost 5, so that sounds just about perfect, I'll check them out.

replies.title: Re: Chapter 4 of Seed of Yggdrasill and Question on a "Shared Soul"
replies.content:
When I read "Soul of the Gods," I liken it to my understanding of Hestia. She's a goddess of the home hearth, but also of the celestial hearth. The home hearth is where the gods are honored and they have their places upon it. The celestial hearth is the spiritual firmament in which the gods exist and without which they are indistinct.

Not sure how that works with a pantheism or pan-monotheism as it seems you're describing. (I haven't read her work.) Perhaps, Idhun is the string that holds each mask in place.

replies.title: Re: Chapter 4 of Seed of Yggdrasill and Question on a "Shared Soul"
replies.content:
The comparison to Hestia is interesting, and worth thinking about symbolically, if nothing else. That's one area where I find Kvilhaug's approach breaks down for me - it's neither "everything's just a metaphor, so don't worry about what it 'literally' is," nor is it a carefully-worked out theology/metaphysics from what I can tell, but rather something that borrows from each at different times, and not necessarily consistent. But maybe I'm being too literal-minded!

As for the metaphor of the "string that holds each mask in place," thank you! That's quite interesting.

replies.title: Re: Chapter 4 of Seed of Yggdrasill and Question on a "Shared Soul"
replies.content:
This is an interesting question, Jeff, about Idhun being a ‘shared soul’ among the Norse Deities. I haven’t read much of the material you’re referring to, and I can’t say that I grasp the overall worldview within which that statement was made. As far as I can tell by what you write, it’s so different from mine that my thoughts may not have much value here, but I’ll add a bit to the discussion anyway!

I agree with some comments here that it’s too bad we don’t know the original word Kvilhaug was using for ‘soul.’ The Norwegian/ Scandinavian word most often used for ‘soul’ today, ‘sal’, was actually borrowed from Anglo-Saxon ‘shawl…this blasted spellchecker won’t let me spell it right, it’s shawl without the ‘h’…anyway, borrowed from A-S because Norse apparently didn’t have a native word that was satisfactory for Christian purposes.

According to my researches, there are multiple words and concepts for what we call ‘soul’ in the old Germanic / Norse languages, so your question becomes yet more complex: which kind of soul is Kvilhaug really talking about? Some of the souls, like the Hugr, are very individualistic and clearly wouldn’t work here. There are a couple that could work, though.

The most obvious one, to me, is the Fjor (with an umlaut) / Ferah / Feorh. This soul is the expression of the life-force within a person or being; it takes on personality but is nevertheless a soul-substrate that is shared with other living beings. Old Norse has the plural word Fjarg that refers to all the Deities and is based on Fjor. Germanic words derived from the singular word such as Firihi refer to the folk as a whole, as well as the Feorhcynn, the kindred of the living.

The word Fjor and its relatives are also used for species of trees, especially oak, pine, and fir, but also trees in general, a link with Idhun and her apples. The name of the Indo-European Thunder-God, Perkwunos, comes from this word, as well as the Proto-Indo-European word for the life-soul, perkwus. Humans were formed from trees or logs in our myths, and I’ve retold that story from the point of view of this soul in my writings.

So we have a soul here that is closely related to our Deities in the plural, to trees, and to both individual and collective expressions of the life-force. This could be made to work in the way that Kvilhaug suggests, but I have no idea if that is really what she’s referring to. I come from pretty much the opposite pole, viewing not only the Deities as individuals, but also positing multiple individual souls per person and per Deity. But the meditations you suggest would no doubt be fruitful, regardless!

Winifred Hodge Rose

replies.title: Re: Chapter 4 of Seed of Yggdrasill and Question on a "Shared Soul"
replies.content:
Thanks very much for your thoughts, Winifred! Your suggestions will certainly deepen any meditation I do on the topic.

I've been writing up Seed of Yggdrasill chapter-by-chapter, but at the end of it, I might see if I can do an as-simple-as-possible restatement of Kvilhaug's thinking and positions, if for no other reason than so that I can know for sure how much of it I agree with or not!

Thanks again,
Jeff

comments.title: Dodging eclipses
comments.content:
It so happens that I have a very challenging month ahead of me. I must move myself and family by a strict and inflexible deadline, under such conditions that one might fairly say we must move mountains to complete the necessary tasks on time. Much hangs on this.

It has come to my attention that a solar and a lunar eclipse are fast approaching, one on the fourteenth of the month and the other on the twenty-eighth, if memory serves. Reading and experience alike have taught me to be leery of eclipses. The last thing I need at such a critical time is for discordant celestial energies to come bolluxing up my already difficult tasks. Can you suggest any measures I might take to protect myself from their influences?

I have experience with basic HOGD magic, using Hellenic deities and mythology.

replies.title: Re: Dodging eclipses
replies.content: Fortunately eclipses are brief, and only have an effect on your personal life while they are in process. (They have a prolonged effect on nations, but that's a matter for mundane astrology.) Avoid doing any ceremonial magic during the interval when the eclipse is actually happening, and avoid beginning any important activity during that same interval; other than that, don't worry about it.

replies.title: Re: Dodging eclipses
replies.content: Thanks! I’ll get exact times on Astrodienst, and enjoy a quiet interval while they’re going on.

replies.title: Re: Dodging eclipses
replies.content:
Not to disagree with you, but I am checking astrology sites online and none of them say a word on eclipses in general being maleficent. They actually encourage people to welcome the eclipse energies, for astrologically aligned purposes!!!

The one article with a more nuanced approach (Sage Goddess' Rare New Moon Annular Solar Eclipse) recommended waiting 48 hours after the eclipse has passed because "eclipses shake things up and bring new awareness, we need time to receive and process them first". Which is good advice, but not 100% honest about the reasons behind this pause time, if I understand correctly.

Is this just New Age types being new-agey, or you think other developments are at play?

replies.title: Re: Dodging eclipses
replies.content: New Age crap - traditionally, eclipses are ALWAYS malefic. There are lots of rituals you can do during the eclipses, but they're mainly to ward off the negative effects.

replies.title: Re: Dodging eclipses
replies.content: I will say that I had a lot of personal planetary activations around the taurus - scorpio eclipses over the last 18 months and they weren't really bad, just major shifts in my life.

replies.title: Re: Dodging eclipses
replies.content: One of the disagreements I have with the last half century or so of pop astrologers is that they've forgotten that anything can be malefic. (Except for Mercury retrogrades, that is, which many of them treat as The Worst Thing Evah.) Eclipses are traditionally malefic. That doesn't mean they're always a source of disaster -- as ever, it depends on how they affect your natal chart, and also on the aspects made by the eclipse to other planets -- but like Mars, Saturn, and Uranus, the malefic planets, they're always challenging to deal with.

replies.title: Re: Dodging eclipses
replies.content:
To piggyback on this question. What would be the consequences of having been born during an eclipse? Are there differences if it is Lunar or Solar? What about C-section vs natural childbirth?

I am asking because a new baby nephew is due to this October. I am rooting for the 17th for date when he will be born, but it is uncomfortably close to the Solar Eclipse on the 14th...

replies.title: Re: Dodging eclipses
replies.content: I'm not an expert in natal astrology, so you'd probably have to ask someone more familiar with that branch of the art.

replies.title: Re: Dodging eclipses
replies.content:
Fair enough, thank you! Since this is something that we have little or no control over, maybe the answer is "whatever this child's karma will bring on". On reading the OP, my mind jumped to the significance of Comets, which has its own league of dark implications. Now I can tell where I was wrong.
From your previous answer, comparing this with the influence of Mars or Saturn in the natal chart makes sense; and it adds a lot of perspective as well. Having a fair bit of Martian energy myself, I can tell how this can be both a challenge and an opportunity.

replies.title: Re: Dodging eclipses
replies.content: In Vedic astrology this can be a serious problem called a "Grahana Dosha" which causes sickness, suffering and poverty. Being born on the exact new moon is also a dosha, causing similar problems. There are traditional rituals (Puja) that one can have a Vedic priest do which alleviates the problem.

comments.title: Gets
comments.content:
JMG,

Please could I ask if you know of any precedents for the phenomenon of 'gets' (repeated digits that appear more often than they randomly should and appear to have some significance or meaning associated with them) from before the 2016 USA election campaign?

replies.title: Re: Gets
replies.content: Only in a very general sense. Occultists and mystics for centuries have noticed that meaningful coincidences are a helpful indication that spiritual forces are involved in a situation; Carl Jung's concept of synchronicity put that same insight into modern terms. Some people have reported that certain meaningful numbers come up for them repeatedly in certain situations, in ways that are hard to account for by chance. The specific phenomenon of "gets" as it appeared on the Chans in the era of Kek is one form of this broader pattern -- a unique form, as far as I know, but a form that makes sense within the context.

replies.title: Re: Gets
replies.content: Thank-you

comments.title: Financial divination
comments.content:
Hi JMG, I asked before about the use of divination to augment a short-term investing strategy - predicting whether hour-by-hour movements in a financial market would continue or reverse. Someone mentioned that such a use of magic was treading close to the pitfall of trying to get something for nothing.

Do you have an opinion on this? I see it as similar to a farmer or sailor using divination to determine what kind of weather is on the way. From my perspective a market can be seen as a natural emergent system, like the tide going in and out. Determining the course of such forces of nature has been one of the most ancient uses of magic and divination, and my divining for this purpose must be complemented by my own skills and understanding to be productive. I have trouble seeing it as being comparable to someone who wishes for millions of dollars to fall from the sky.

Thanks again for these posts.

replies.title: Re: Financial divination
replies.content:
My (non-JMG) initial two cents on this... compare short-term trading to your other examples. A sailor or a farmer who successfully uses divination has, say, prevented a disaster or produced a more bountiful harvest. There are individual winners and losers from that outcome (e.g., their direct competitors), but overall society is richer by having more "stuff" regardless of the specific distribution

With short-term trading, there is a nonzero amount of value created by helping make prices incrementally more accurate. That is a very, very small amount, though. The vast majority of your gains and losses are just the flip side of other people's experience. While most other participants probably understand and accept that dynamic at some level, not all of them do.

Maybe "getting something for nothing" isn't 100% the best way to characterize it, as there could be some level of required skill/knowledge involved. Regardless, it is pretty close to a zero-sum game.

replies.title: Re: Financial divination
replies.content: Divination is not magic. People have been using divination to guide economic decisions since about an hour after divination was invented, and a lot of day traders in the stock market rely on astrology; it apparently works tolerably well. I wouldn't expect this to be any trouble at all.

replies.title: Re: Financial divination
replies.content:
I don't have the link handy, but to be clear, I think I suggested to not divine questions that were backed by an intention of "which investment decisions will get me money?", but to instead divine questions that were backed by an intention more like "which investment decisions will increase wealth?". That would work on the same principle on which everyone else goes along with letting mundane skillful investing decisions happen because they increase wealth. (And then the problem of when or whether more wealth would be actually good can be handled by a different part of the system with different comparative advantages; though eventually you should look into your own personal responsibilities in relation to that question as well.)

I expect that this changed choice of questions would require you to voluntarily soak up losses sometimes, the same way an investor takes losses at one time to have more wealth later, or a nation buys up some of its fiat currency sometimes to have the national wealth increase later. You'd be effectively becoming an instrument of a force you only understand in outline, which would be using your investment funds pool as working capital. But maybe these losses would be "better" than the amount by which, or circumstances under which, you would suffer losses due to karma and/or raspberry jam under the other plan.

There is totally an element of effect where causing money to be lost by people whose money-backed predictions make market-derived production decisions worse (whether absolutely or just relatively) can be positive, so long as their money then goes to people whose money-backed predictions make market-derived production decisions better (which doesn't have to mean it'll be you).

There's a continuum between regular value investing and lotteries, and day trading seems like it would have to be somewhere in between. I don't know what to say about the finding that JMG mentions about how financial astrology empirically seems to be okay in connection with day trading. Maybe it's okay because it is more about larger-scale market movements, and these correspond to corrections generally? Maybe the problem is that there's just something wrong with my supposition that there's a continuum between the "magical" intention of, "please make real a timeline where my speculation is profitable, agnostically as to whether or not this 'changes' what speculation I make", and the "divinatory" intention of, "please make real a timeline where my speculation is profitable, but mostly by 'changing' what speculation I make (by 'changing' what judgement my oracle produces)". (Consider what this distinction would have to look like in the context of an actual, explicitly conceived lottery.)

In any case, if you have a divination procedure that you trust in relation to trading decisions, maybe you should see if it can be adapted to telling you about where the line is between value investing and lotteries, and how fuzzy the line is.

I don't know of a good, economically literate religious or mythological treatment of this sphere of effects, to try to add an element of symbolic investment to your practice here. I don't know if this will help, but, among internet rationalists, there has been a small profusion of collaborative fiction in the default setting of the D&D-derived RPG Pathfinder, which among other things treats these effects as part of the deeper structure of the purview of the deity Abadar. RPG deities are like the child's doll version of real deities, right? Canonically Abadar is the Lawful Neutral god of cities, civilization, commerce, the rule of law, culture, wealth, and stability, whose clerics get special spells for verifying statements and fair valuation. Fanonically, these are the mortal-realm manifestations of deeper divine Lawful principles whose more abstract intelligible manifestations are things like comparative advantage and specialization and economies of scale and the wealth they yield and enable, Pareto improvements resulting from fairly negotiated precommitments, and institutional quality. The fanon version of Abadar's church uses prediction markets in a way that other religious dimly understand (in a way that mostly gets it backwards) as 'they somehow use gambling as a form of divination'.

Hi JMG,

I've only become open to the idea of magic and the esoteric tradition in the past 12 months, after shaking myself out of hardliner atheist materialism a decade ago. I went through a self-imposed tough time in life from my early 20s through my early 30s, which had no cause but my own inner mental trash. I don't know how to describe it beyond saying that "life was not working". I felt like I was drifting, with no purpose and no hope, and anxiety made it impossible for me to see any possibility of change. At the time I wrote it off as youth believing that surely it would change.

Even after the worst I was left with this nagging feeling that things just aren't "working" and aren't meant to work for me. My life today is not quite the smoking crater, but only just so, even though looking in from outside you might not think it. I still have this nagging feeling that things aren't working and aren't meant to work. For example, starting new projects or employment, or even achieving smaller life-goals. It's much more likely that this is a result of karma from one or more previous lives. Karma usually acts through character, of which your own inner mental trash is part. comments.title: Challenging Geomancy Topic
comments.content:
Hi, JMG. I would like your view on a geomancy chart that I have recently cast. I find it a bit challenging because of the nature of the question. In brief, there is an anti-government social movement that is presently unfolding. My question is whether the movement will benefit for the people of my country.

The four mothers are:
M1: Populus
M2: Laetitia
M3: Conjunctio
M4: Acquisitio

The court that is generated by these figures are:
Right Witness: Puella
Left Witness: Rubeus
Judge: Via

My reading of the court is that this movement will create a transition from cooperation and harmony (Puella) to confrontation and conflict (Rubeus): in general, this does not look good for the people of my country.

Now, I am of the habit of interpreting both the court and the house chart. Given the nature of the question, I took the 1st house to represent the citizens of my country. The figure happens to be Populus. I see it as representing a unified mass of people (as an aside, in a scrying that I did years ago on Populus I saw a monument commemorating the townspeople overthrowing a local tyrant through their show of unity) and, so, I believe that this is a somewhat favourable figure for this question.

I also paid attention to a few other houses and to their figures and any interaction with each other and with the 1st house. I looked at the 9th house, as there is both a religious and legal/ethics aspect to this movement. The figure is the highly favourable figure of Laetitia, which I take to mean that they are making swift gains and feel empowered and/or that they are on the right side. Laetitia passes to the 2nd house and therefore perfects by conjunction with the 1st house.

As the movement is anti-government, I figured that it makes sense to look at the 10th house. Placed there is the strong figure Fortuna Major, which I take to mean that the government is not seriously threatened by the movement. Fortuna Major also perfects with the 1st house, but it is placed in the inauspicious 12th house: I take this to mean that the government may use traitors/spies to discredit the movement in the eyes of the public; given the nature of the figure, it has me concerned.

Now for the twist: Fortuna Major also appears in the 6th house, which is square Laetitia in the 9th house and therefore shows an opposing or blocking force to the hopes indicated in the 9th house. Now, the 6th is also the house of the working class – and this movement also has a strong ‘blue collar’ element to it. This is what throws me off, as I see the blue collar element to be the least likely group to be corrupted by the government -- though it may be possible for them to be ‘duped’ by government-allied (or funded) agents of disinformation, sowers of discord, or architects of a ‘trap’ (12th house stuff).

The cardines are fairly favourable: Populus (I), Fortuna Major (X), Albus (VII) and Acquisitio (IV), indicating that the public could gain from the situation. The Reconciler is Via.

So, there we have it: my take-home is that the movement will change the ‘tone’ of anti-government activities to being less peaceful (and may face harsh government opposition) and despite the moral and legal justification of the movement, the public as a whole will not be significantly affected by it – but, if they are affected, it will be to more fully unify them which could be beneficial.

Sorry it’s so long, but I figure that by giving a full interpretation, it will give you more points to agree or disagree with. This is a hard chart for me to be objective about and I cast it because intuitively I have been getting ‘mixed signals’ about the whole thing; hence I am reaching out to you. Thanks!

Ron M

replies.title: Re: Challenging Geomancy Topic
replies.content:
Okay, I've just drawn up the chart. The first thing I notice is that the chart perfects, but it's not the querent (the movement you're asking about) that does the perfecting. Since the goal of the movement is to change things in your country, it's a 10th house question; you have Fortuna Major n the 10th, which suggests success but it's going to take a while. Fortuna Major perfects by passing to the 12th, into conjunction with Populus in the 1st, while Acquisitio also makes a translation between the houses of the querent and quesited via the 2nd and 9th houses. What this means is that success will come by way of outside factors, possibly not even in Canada (the 9th represents countries at a distance), and by changes in government and institutions (10th and 12th house); there's also something going on in the 6th, which may mean public health, the laboring classes, or the armed forces. In addition, keep an eye on economic factors (2nd house) and legal cases (9th house).

The court gives you a snapshot of your current situation. Puella as right witness says that the movement is idealistic but naive, and has less power than it thinks. Rubeus as left witness warns that you don't actually know what you're up against and there's much more deception and treachery than you think. Via thus indicates that it's going to be a long road -- but then that's implied by Fortuna Major, which is usually difficulty at the beginning followed by success.

That's how I would interpret this chart, at least. Your mileage may vary.

comments.content: Hi JMG. I would like to ask a question about your "temple technology" hypothesis. I didn't understand why Roman Catholic church wanted to suppress this technology in the Middle Ages. I mean, instead of suppressing it, they could have usurped it for their own benefit (to increase crop yields in their fields), and add some Christian flavor to its lore to cover its pre-Christian origins. It doesn't seem like a dangerous heresy for the church. Why did they resort to suppress it so violently?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Nah, you've got the history garbled. The medieval Catholic church eagerly took up the temple technology, starting in the Dark Ages -- Irish monks spread the basic principles all over Europe, which is why so many old European churches are perfect examples of the technology in action. The somewhat limited knowledge the Irish monks had of the method was then greatly increased by knowledge from the Arab world brought back by the Knights Templar; while the Templars were suppressed in the early 14th century, other monastic orders had picked up the same trick, and so had the guilds of stonemasons that built the Gothic churches, many of which are also fine examples of the temple technology.

It wasn't until the Reformation and the Counter-Reformation of the late 16th century that the technology was rejected, and that was done as much by the Protestants as the Catholics. The Protestants rejected everything but bare faith in the Bible as so much superstitious claptrap -- they were the spiritual ancestors of our modern rationalists -- and destroyed the monasteries in the countries where they took power, thus eliminating the institutional framework through which the temple technology was passed down. The process in Catholicism was much more complex, but it had the same effect; after the Council of Trent monasteries and churches across the Catholic world were placed under much stricter surveillance than before, and forced to conform to a fixed set of teachings and rituals handed down by Rome; the Curia apparently didn't know a thing about the temple technology, and the increasingly rigid dogma imposed on local churches squeezed out the necessary knowledge base one monastery at a time.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
From what I've found with my research into the tradition, the Jesuits picked up some knowledge of it, and practiced it in France until at least the 1930s, and possibly still today. What they may have done is pick it up from existing rural traditions, rather than any formal doctrine of the church.

By practicing I mean the dowsing component of the tradition, the Jesuits certainly weren't building any great stone structures, but they may have preserved some.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That's very plausible, because the temple tradition was never part of the formal doctrine -- it was passed on at the monasteries from elder brother to novice, as part of the same kind of craft traditions you also find in, say, monastic brandy making.

Good day, JMG.

As it happens, I'm working through the introspection I mentioned last week.

It seems to me that for every emotion, there is a different response depending on what part of myself is taking the hit. Sometimes it is me, the one who thinks these words, who feels something and the emotion is manifested as a physical change in the body, a shot of hormones that modifies heartbeat and other nasty effects.

Even when I am not feeling like it, if my projection 'should be feeling' something, I have to make the projection react accordingly. This one is rarely a problem, since I am rather honest.

A second insight about how to redirect the energies showed me that I should never go directly against the direction of the force. If I want to punch someone, I don't have to waste my energies holding the force, that is repression and that extenuates me. I should apply tangential force towards a target that is not directly related to my goals, either, so the force goes in the direction of my goals.
So, instead of punching someone's face who is limiting me, I'd take actions that would set myself as a more knowledgeable person in some other field, and eventually this would help me overcome the limits.

Not bad for a week's work.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
On improving one's karma.

Pick one thing that needs to be changed, and change it.

1. Imagine all the things that you may be doing wrong (in the abstract, do not dwell much on any of them). Make peace with the fact that you probably are so wrong about some things that you cannot even notice it. Let them be for the time being.
2. Think of the things that you are aware you are wrong (or reasonably certain that you are wrong). Make peace with the fact that some of those you lack the power to change. Let them be...
3. Focus on the things that you know are doing wrong, and that you could do something about. Find a handful you are reasonably sure you can succeed at. Pick the one that feels will make your life easier if fixed. Fix that one, let the others be...
4. Once you fix the one, you will find it easier to go back to 3 and fix another one.
5. After you fix a few solvable ones, you will notice you can come up with new ways to move stuff that was unsolvable to the solvable bucket. Do so and repeat.
6. Keep going and you will gain awareness of stuff that needs to change but you did not even notice the first time around. Check if it is solvable or not and put it in the correct bucket. Repeat.
7. In the seventh day, the Lord rested. Feel free to stop and let things settle once in a while. Just remember that there will always come another Monday, and that we never run out of things to improve in ourselves.

comments.title: Heimdall

Hi JMG,

I was looking for a picture of the Norse god Heimdall the other day, and came across this one:
https://1.bp.blogspot.com/-BHi2OQwhTd8/V4eRInVgp2I/AAAAAAAAVy4/X-c6nszs1BsD7Z9xCTkZmY0V-KD_FwdpACLcB/s1600/heimdall.jpg

replies.title: Re: Heimdall
replies.content:
[IMAGE:0]

First, what a lovely image! Thank you.

Second, this image was heavily influenced by Renaissance culture, which borrowed from the Romans the idea that all Pagan gods and goddesses could be equated to their Roman equivalents. I don't know if the syncretism ever went beyond that literary habit, but it wouldn't surprise me if some of the Scandinavian Rosicrucians like Johannes Bureus got into that.

replies.title: Re: Heimdall
replies.content:
I don't know that I've run into any explicit conflation/identification of Heimdall with Mercury. Way back around the first Roman contact with the Germans, they identified Mercury with Odin, presumably because of the wandering, magic, and psychopomp roles.

Reasons I can think a Renaissance illustrator might go for Mercury when illustrating Heimdall: 1) his primary description in the Prose Edda is as the "gatekeeper of the Gods," which has a similar liminal role, 2) the Gjallarhorn used to announce danger to the Gods is a kind of "message," 3) Heimdall changes shape (into a seal to fight Loki) in at least one story.

Other than that, it's a rather interesting decision for attributes, as far as I can tell.

replies.title: Re: Heimdall
replies.content:
As far as I’m aware, there would be more of a connection of Heimdall and his Gjallarhorn with the angel Gabriel and his blowing of the trumpet for the Day of Judgement. I don’t know whether ther might be any connection in esoteric symbolism with Heimdall’s birth from nine mothers?

Winifred Hodge Rose

replies.title: Re: Heimdall
replies.content: In modern magical symbolism, both of those would equate him with Yesod, the ninth sphere of the Tree of Life, and thus with the Moon -- but the Renaissance didn't use modern magical symbolism. They had their own ideas!

comments.title: Qs
comments.content:
Greetings everyone!

Some questions for JMG (open to everyone):

1) I am in the midst of a kundalini rising experience, and in my research I found some notes/recommendations by people who suggested that its important to keep the kundalini energy grounded, and not let too much of it constantly coalesce around the head because it has an unbalancing effect, and causes sociopathy etc, giving the example of Osho and other gurus where abuse or some type of extreme dangerous behavior was later found to be rampant.

To head heavy thinkers its probably very tempting to be in this heady cognitive state because of the stimulus it provides, but it clearly causes delusions (I noticed i was having inflationary thoughts when i was having a lot of energy gather in my head, which caused me to start working on grounding the energy in my body more).

The recommendation was to work on the heart centers to balance this side effect. What are you thoughts on this and do you have any other recommendations to keep this process balanced and not end up with massive ego inflation?

2)Could you explain from your learnings how karma as our own choice squares up with free will, esp if the principle of sufficient reason operates on the material plane? I know Schopenhauer made some arguments about a "transcendental will" but would love to hear your understanding on how the possible integration of the spiritual and material works.

replies.title: Re: Qs
replies.content:
1) Anything that helps ground you on the material plane will help you stay balanced. Rather than the heart center, which can lead to its own kind of inflation, I recommend work with the solar plexus center and the womb center/hara/dantien. Oh, and bacon cheeseburgers. Meat helps get us back in our bodies, which after all are made of meat.

2) Most of the confusions about free will and determinism come from the habit of insisting that human existence has to be all one way or all the other -- wholly free or wholly determined. Very clearly, our existence is some of each. As I see it, human beings have the potential for free will, but it's just a potential until it's developed. Those in whom it's undeveloped are as predictable as machines; in those who've begun to develop it, it takes the form of a little wiggle room here and there; and it expands from there.

Karma is one of the things that has to be cleared away so that free will can develop, since as long as you're burdened by karma, that sharply limits your freedom of will. Free will can never be more than partial during material incarnation, in part precisely because of the power of the principle of sufficient reason, but that principle isn't absolute or omnipotent; the random, the whimsical, and the absurd are also inherent parts of our existence -- as the Existentialists grasped, though Schopenhauer didn't. (His philosophy would have benefited immensely from more of a capacity to laugh.)

William Walker Atkinson was a major figure in the New Thought movement but he was also an occultist, a Theosophist, and a pioneer in adapting Hindu ideas to a Western setting. comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Hi JMG. Why did the Jacobins wear Phrygian caps (which is an Orphic symbol)? Did they have any relation with esotericism? (I know that many of the Jacobins were Freemasons, but AFAIK, they just used Masonic lodges for secret political gatherings.)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
In ancient Rome, slaves who were freed were given a particular style of felt cap as an emblem of liberty. In the 18th century that got confused with the Phrygian cap, and revolutionaries in the American colonies adopted it as an emblem, as part of the same classical fanboy/fangirl attitude that gave us a Senate and all those government buildings in white marble with pillars. The Jacobins, who were crazed fans of the American revolutionaries, borrowed it from there.

Masonry was more than a location for meetings, though. The American revolution was largely launched and coordinated by Masonic lodges -- if you read the minutes of one of the old Boston lodges, on the night of the Boston Tea Party, the lodge minutes say, "The meeting was suspended due to pressing business elsewhere." Simon Bolivar, who liberated Latin America from the Spanish, was also a Mason and used his Masonic connections in the struggle for liberty. The Jacobins were part of the same pattern. Masonry's commitment to the idea of human equality and liberty made it a very useful framework for such projects -- which is why Hitler, Lenin, and the Ayatollah Khomeini all banned Masonry from their respective countries as soon as they seized power.

comments.title: Boundaries, Perception, Self: a model
comments.content:
JMG and the commentariat:

Have been mulling over the idea of boundaries, trying to make sense of a few things, and sketched out a basic model:

We have two major boundaries: The boundary separating self from perception, and the boundary separating mundane material perception from... "the neighborhood". All the regular stuff you perceive with the senses, comprehend with the intellect, that stuff is in your house but it's not *you*, and then there's outside, the neighborhood.

The self is... the self.
Perception is squishier-- its boundaries can be shrunk or expanded. You can go blind. You can hunker down in a narrow worldview and refuse to see anything outside it. You can study and learn new things, you can meditate and practice askesis or hesychasm and push that boundary outward. You can even crowbar it open with drugs (though this seems foolish-- boundaries exist for a reason).

The neighborhood is everything else-- probably has its own borders but functionally we will never find them in this life, so it's immaterial.

Why not just bust open all the boundaries and join the worldsoul? Because we're human and we can't function that way.

I submit that a porous border between self and perception results in emotional instability-- you can't separate your own self, and ideas and emotions that you are perceiving, so you wash about one way and the other, cry at movies, get swept up fads, angry at every clickbait outrage invitation, that sort of thing.

If you've got a reasonably functional self border, but a porous perceptual border, the result is constant overwhelming input. Not enough filter-- stuff from the neighborhood comes wandering into your house to pester you, it's like living in an apartment where the walls are thin and the neighbors are noisy. Old Hippie syndrome, perhaps.

If both borders are porous, that's schizophrenia. You can hear everything going on in the neighborhood, *and* you can't separate it from your self.

The neighborhood-- is variable. 2 examples to clarify:
a) Faust asks the devil why he can be here for a chat if devils aren't allowed out of hell. Haha! says the devil, we've never left hell, you came to us!
b) "Blessed are the pure in heart, for they shall see God".

These are both examples of "neighborhood". When you open the doors of your perception, what's out there? It depends on what neighborhood you're living in, and you do have some control over that. Pure in heart? You're living in the same suburb as the angels. The pure in heart can see God because they're living where God is, neh? Meanwhile, if your heart and your habits have got you living in a sketchy neighborhood, maybe you avoid opening the doors at all, because what's out there isn't nice.

So...

1) Am I reinventing the wheel here? Surely others, smarter than me, have explored here-- if you've already read them, can I get book recommendations?

2) Pull this model apart and tell me where I got it wrong so I can rework/understand it better. What elephant did I miss while cataloguing crickets?

3) I have the sense that the boundaries of perception are influenced by culture. Any idea why modern industrial/tech culture seems so determined to clamp them down to the smallest possible perimeter? Have the woods gotten more dangerous or what?

replies.title: Re: Boundaries, Perception, Self: a model
replies.content: This is fascinating. I don't know of anyone who's focused on modeling the self/environment relationship in terms of more or less permeable boundaries, though I haven't read that extensively in cognitive science. I don't see any immediate flaws in it but I'd have to brood over it for a while and see if anything comes to mind.

replies.title: Re: Boundaries, Perception, Self: a model
replies.content:
It's only partially a cognitive thing-- I'd relate it also to ideas about, say, clairvoyance. Like, what's the difference between a schizo who hears absolutely everything, a saint to whom your heart and the future are an open book, and an old hippie negotiating with his persistent hallucinations?

Is it possible that it's a fairly simple question of boundaries? The schizo has none. The hippie maybe has an intact self and a leaky perceptual boundary, having failed to attend to his neighborhood before bashing open the doors of perception looking for chemical enlightenment. The saint? He's followed the path of humility and ascesis, the guidance of monks who came before him, moved to a good neighborhood, and then thrown open the doors of his house/perception and gotten to know his neighbors...?

In this model, any kind of clairvoyance would be about the perceptual boundary, and there'd be important differences between whether you'd pushed that boundary out consciously with training and practice (meditation, fasting, the guidance of a teacher), or the boundary was just porous for whatever reason and you hadn't much control over it... and what sort of neighborhood you lived in would be the main determining factor in what you could see.

--waits for someone to tell me the huge thing I'm missing-- I'm not that smart ;)

replies.title: Re: Boundaries, Perception, Self: a model
replies.content: You're smarter than you let yourself admit.

replies.title: Re: Boundaries, Perception, Self: a model
replies.content:
Charles Taylor discusses what he calls the "buffered self" in A Secular Age. By this he's indicating the anomalous way that recent-ish Western culture has come to understand personal identity. Most cultures through most of history held a more "porous" notion of identity that didn't fully separate the self from the world/cosmos/reality. Since the Renaissance, give or take, the greater Western civilization has come to "wall up" ourselves inside a private mind and segregate it from everything else.

It's been awhile since I read those parts of the book, but IIRC his discussion is not too far from something like Owen Barfield's ideas on participation. It also ties in to the discussion of Weberian disenchantment.

replies.title: Re: Boundaries, Perception, Self: a model
replies.content:
Okay, this is slightly embarrassing to bring up in this context, but it's one of the only things that came to mind. A D&D blogger I used to read regularly did a thought experiment about how beings with different kinds of psychic powers would perceive the boundary of the self.

The idea is that if it's just as easy to move your hand with muscles as it is to move a broom with your brain, then why isn't the broom just as much a "part of you"? Likewise, if other folks' thoughts are just as apparent and obvious as your own, would you really think of them as "other people thinking" or as "weird intrusions into my mental space."

Now, I wanted to explain before linking for a couple of reasons. 1) The blog is called "D&D with Pornstars" because this blogger and most of his friends are, in fact, porn actors. That means the whole blog is tagged with an adult content filter, but really, it's 99.99% dorky D&D stuff, with the occasional nude painting (the author is also a professional painter), 2) the author, Zak Smith, known as Zak Sabbath, was the center of a big ol' kerfuffle in certain corners of the D&D world, and because of that, there's been a lot of drama about him/around him/involving him, so that's a further source of "this is awkward." Very likely no one here particularly cares, 3) the author is a self-described materialist hedonist marxist (I think he literally said sex was the highest good you can aspire to), so take his pseudo-philosophical musings with an appropriate dose of salt, and also, remember that he was running this thought experiment for the sake of coming up with interesting scenarios for D&D players to encounter!

Anyhow, with all that out of the way, the link: https://dndwithpornstars.blogspot.com/2014/02/the-philosophers.html

replies.title: Re: Boundaries, Perception, Self: a model
replies.content: From memory, Carl du Prel saw the boundaries of perception not as being porous, but rather as moveable, and that the shifting of the boundaries of perception for the individual is what explained things like visions etc.

replies.title: Re: Boundaries, Perception, Self: a model
replies.content:
These are intriguing thoughts. I've thought and meditated on boundaries and have some thoughts that may be food for further exploration.

One train of thought came while examining others who had gone "all-in" with The Secret and that sort of thinking (we create the world from our thoughts, just think and be rich!) Perception is an equivalent force to intention. If you clear intention but are completely delusional, you will have trouble. I think of the power of intention like Pacman, you can hold your control stick in the direction you want to go, and you will have to wait until all the other forces align so you can actually move that way. To apply to your thoughts: if you can control what another perceives you can clamp down on intentions you want to dissuade; if you can perceive clearly, then you can flex your intentions effectively.

You speak of porous borders of perception that are impediments; perhaps though, it's not their porosity that is the impediment, but rather the clarity of where that border lies - the problem is relying on a border that doesn't exist or not understanding the border between self and other.

Another thought from previous work with modern heathen philosophy. In our neo-liberal/neo-conservative neo-present, we are taught to rely on society, to dissolve our borders of self and community and make all-of-society (or even all-of-world) into our community- essentially dissolving all borders. By "alienating" some groups into "others," we can regain what it means to have borders, and actually relate to both those in the trusted "in-group" and the "out-group" more authentically.

Thank you for voicing these thoughts. It helped me to coalesce some threads into something more resembling the tapestry I hoped to be weaving. ;)

May you be blessed in the week to come.

Randomness is not always required!

Is there some sort of working or prayer or something I could do for a god to 'choose me' so to speak - like to send out some kind of 'I'm opening and willing' message and wait to see if there is a response?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Finding a deity is rather like finding a spouse -- it's very rarely something you can just go out there and do! What I recommend is that you start by reading mythology -- choose collections from many different traditions. (The young adult section of your local public library is often a good place to get these.) Read them, think about them, see which stories and which deities catch your attention and call to you. When you've become attracted to a deity, learn as much as you can about them, find or make an image, set up a little shrine someplace private, and start praying to them. Don't expect instant results. Simply make the deity part of your life, and let the relationship unfold.
